body:
### Security intent

This control validates that **there is only one active access key available for any single IAM user** across AWS.

### Access condition reviewed

The assessment focuses on iAM users with multiple active access keys. It reviews the identity, credential, role, or policy state that creates the access condition described by the control.

### Practitioner note

Identity findings deserve careful cleanup rather than blind deletion. Confirm ownership and business need, remove unnecessary standing access, and prefer federation, groups, roles, MFA, and short-lived credentials over direct long-lived grants.

This control maps to a benchmark-style requirement, so the expected state should match the benchmark wording unless your organization has an approved exception.
name: Ensure there is only one active access key available for any single IAM user
remediationDescription:
**From Console**

Perform the following action to deactivate access keys:

1. Sign into the AWS console as an **Administrator** and navigate to the [IAM Dashboard](https://console.aws.amazon.com/iam/home#/home).
2. In the left navigation pane, choose Users.
3. Click on the **User name** for which more than one active access key exists.
4. Click on **Security credentials** tab.
5. Click on the **Make inactive** to `deactivate` the non-operational key.

**Note**: Test your application to make sure that the active access key is working.

**From Command Line**

Run the `update-access-key` command below using the IAM user name and the non-operational access key IDs to deactivate the unnecessary key.

```bash
aws iam update-access-key --access-key-id <accessKeyID> --status Inactive --user-name <username>
```
description: IAM users with multiple active access keys
